definition: Rejecting a claim by attacking the character, motive, or traits of the person making it rather than the claim itself.
example: "You can't trust her budget analysis — she's never even run a business."
lookFor: An insult or label aimed at the speaker
A shift from the argument to the arguer's identity or motives
Words like 'of course he'd say that'
commonConfusions: Tu Quoque, which specifically accuses the person of hypocrisy
Legitimate questioning of a relevant expert's credentials

definition: Presenting two options as the only possibilities when others exist.
example: "Either we cut the whole program or we go bankrupt."
lookFor: 'Either / or' with no middle ground
'If you're not with us, you're against us'
Reasonable alternatives quietly excluded
commonConfusions: Straw Man, which distorts a position rather than the option set
A genuine situation where only two options really do exist

definition: Relying on a personal or isolated story in place of representative evidence.
example: "My uncle smoked his whole life and was fine, so the warnings are overblown."
lookFor: 'I know someone who...'
A single dramatic case used to overturn broad data
Personal experience presented as proof of a general trend
commonConfusions: Hasty Generalization, which is about sample size more than narrative
An illustrative example used alongside real evidence

definition: Claiming something is true or right because many people believe or do it.
example: "Everyone is switching to this app, so it must be the best one."
lookFor: 'Everyone knows / everyone is doing it'
Popularity offered as evidence of truth or quality
Pressure not to be the odd one out
commonConfusions: Appeal to Pity or Fear, which use other emotions
Citing genuine expert consensus, which is different from mere popularity

definition: Misrepresenting someone's position to make it easier to attack.
example: "You want to fund the library? So you think we should ignore the roads entirely."
lookFor: An exaggerated or invented version of an opponent's view
'So what you're really saying is...'
Attacking a claim no one actually made
commonConfusions: False Dilemma, which limits options rather than distorting a stance
A fair, accurate restatement of an opposing view

definition: Substituting a threat or frightening image for an actual argument.
example: "Vote for this or your family will not be safe."
lookFor: Vivid danger with no evidence it will happen
Pressure to act now to avoid catastrophe
Emotion doing the work a reason should
commonConfusions: Appeal to Pity, which targets sympathy rather than fear
A legitimate, evidence-based warning about real risk

plainName: After This, Therefore Because of This
definition: Assuming that because one event followed another, the first caused the second.
example: "Sales rose after we changed the logo, so the logo caused the increase."
lookFor: Cause inferred purely from timing
No mention of other possible explanations
'Ever since we did X, Y started happening'
commonConfusions: Correlation/Causation confusion in general
A causal claim that is actually backed by a controlled comparison

definition: Dismissing criticism by pointing out that the critic is guilty of the same thing, rather than addressing the criticism.
example: "You say I'm late, but you were late twice last week."
lookFor: 'What about you / what about them'
Deflecting a charge by accusing the accuser
No engagement with the original point
commonConfusions: Ad Hominem, which attacks character broadly rather than alleging hypocrisy
A fair observation that someone is applying a double standard

definition: Using guilt or sympathy to win a point that the evidence does not support.
example: "You should give me the grade — I studied so hard and I'll lose my scholarship."
lookFor: Hardship offered as a reason to accept a claim
Focus on consequences for the speaker, not the merits
'After everything I've been through...'
commonConfusions: Appeal to Fear, which threatens rather than evokes pity
A relevant appeal to compassion in a genuinely moral decision

definition: Drawing a broad conclusion from a sample that is too small or unrepresentative.
example: "Two coworkers were rude, so this whole company is toxic."
lookFor: A sweeping claim from one or two cases
'Always', 'never', 'everyone' after a tiny sample
No mention of how typical the examples are
commonConfusions: Anecdotal Evidence, which leans on a single vivid story
A well-supported generalization backed by representative data

definition: Treating two things that vary together as proof that one causes the other.
example: "Cities with more bookstores have higher incomes, so bookstores raise income."
lookFor: Two trends moving together presented as cause and effect
No consideration of a shared underlying cause
'X is linked to Y' read as 'X causes Y'
commonConfusions: Post Hoc, which is specifically about time order
A causal claim supported by more than the correlation alone

definition: Using emotionally charged words to push a conclusion in place of argument.
example: "Only a reckless extremist would question this sensible, common-sense plan."
lookFor: Charged adjectives doing persuasive work
Praise or smear words instead of reasons
Wording that pre-decides the verdict
commonConfusions: Appeal to Fear, when the charged words specifically threaten
Strong but accurate description backed by evidence

definition: Introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original issue.
example: "Asked about the budget overrun, the manager began talking about how much overtime the team had been working."
lookFor: A pivot to an unrelated but emotionally charged topic
Avoidance of the actual question or claim
'But what about...' before changing direction
commonConfusions: Tu Quoque, which deflects with hypocrisy specifically
A genuinely related side point introduced honestly

definition: Claiming a small first step will inevitably lead to extreme consequences, with no evidence for the chain.
example: "If we let students retake one quiz, they'll demand do-overs on every test, and grades will become meaningless."
lookFor: A chain of dire predictions from a small action
No mechanism connecting each step to the next
Words like 'before you know it' or 'inevitably'
commonConfusions: Appeal to Fear, which threatens broadly rather than chaining steps
A well-supported causal chain backed by evidence

definition: Restating the conclusion as a premise — assuming what you are supposed to prove.
example: "This policy is the best choice because no better policy exists."
lookFor: A premise that just rephrases the conclusion
Definitions that quietly smuggle in the conclusion
Arguments that move in a small circle
commonConfusions: Bandwagon, which appeals to popularity rather than assuming the conclusion
A definition that supports a claim genuinely independent of the conclusion

definition: Citing an authority whose expertise doesn't apply, or treating their word as final without evidence.
example: "A famous actor swears by this supplement, so it must really work."
lookFor: An expert in a different field used to vouch for the claim
'Doctors recommend' with no study or specifics
Title or fame standing in for evidence
commonConfusions: Bandwagon, which cites a crowd rather than an authority
Legitimate citation of a qualified expert backed by evidence

definition: Selecting only the evidence that supports a claim while ignoring contrary evidence.
example: "Three studies show our product works (ignoring five that found no effect)."
lookFor: Only positive cases highlighted; failures missing
'Our top customers say...' with no overall data
Statistics presented without their counter-examples
commonConfusions: Anecdotal Evidence, which uses one story rather than picking from many
A fair summary that acknowledges contrary evidence

definition: Treating two things as roughly equal in some important way when they really aren't.
example: "Skipping one staff meeting is just as bad as missing a customer deadline."
lookFor: 'Just as bad as' for very different things
Comparisons that ignore differences in scale, impact, or context
Both-sidesism that flattens real distinctions
commonConfusions: Straw Man, which distorts a position rather than comparing two things
An accurate comparison that does match in the relevant way
